Question 3: Should I continue to dollar-cost average into PROG Holdings?

I currently own 107 shares of PROG (Ticker: PRG) at a cost basis of $46.54. Obviously, that is not ideal based on the current stock price of $28.11 (11.22.2025). My goal in answering questions 1 and 2 is to truly evaluate the growth and share appreciation possibilities of PROG. I do not want to blindly add to one of my biggest losers simply to decrease my cost basis. I have no issue selling my position and taking a loss if PROG does not present an opportunity for future returns.

I will continue to dollar-cost average into my existing position to lower my cost basis since I believe PROG’s share price will produce at least a ~95% return over the next 3-5 years. The growth of Four and the value of the share buyback present an opportunity for future returns at today's misaligned stock price of $28.11.

The Dividend

PROG’s dividend is also an important part of management’s capital return strategy. PROG has an annual dividend of $0.52 ($0.13 / quarter). I believe the aforementioned P/E rerating will take time to present itself because it will coincide with Four becoming a larger part of the PROG revenue mix. So being paid a dividend, in addition to share buybacks, provides a floor for this investment. To provide a conservative return estimate, I did not include dividends in my 95% return figure.

Optimistic Thinking

You are most likely interested in PROG if you have made it this far. But please take the following speculation with a grain of salt. Earlier we discussed the sale of Vive Financial by PROG. This sale resulted in $150 million of excess cash on PROG’s balance sheet. I see two ways the $150 million could be deployed:

  1. Accelerated Share Buyback
  2. Accelerate Growth of Four

I do not have an assessment of which of the aforementioned options are more likely. But I will provide some numbers around the share buyback option. PROG has ~$300 million left on their share buyback authorization. Which means Steven Micheals (CEO) technically wouldn’t have to gain approval from his board to execute $150 million in buybacks. The speculated buyback would result in a reduction of outstanding shares by ~13.4% at today’s stock price of $28.11 which would have an immediate positive impact on EPS. Management’s other potential option could be to accelerate the growth of Four. I have absolutely no insight into what amount of capital that would take or what growth rate/ROIC that investment would result in. But I’m sure management will look at every option to capitalize on the growth of Four. So, there are reasons to be very optimistic regarding PROG’s future.

Risks

PROG is sensitive to economic conditions that effect customers’ ability to borrow. This means high-interest rates are a headwind to their main revenue segment, Progressive Leasing, and their fast-growing BNPL segment, Four. When GMV grows, PROG’s revenue directly grows. Unfortunately, when GMV shrinks, PROG’s revenue directly declines. This means PROG’s revenue declines when high-interest rates force their customer base to tighten their spending. Management has not yet given their official 2026 GMV guidance. However, management has stated that they are optimistic about being able to drive GMV growth north of mid-single digits during the Q3 2025 earnings call.

PROG’s share buyback and dividend could be at risk if management incorrectly predicts future GMV growth, which will result in a negative impact on earnings. Luckily, management will provide GMV and revenue guidance during their Q4 2025 earnings call in February, which means we won’t have to wait long to see if we should expect mid-single digit GMV growth for 2026. Also, management has not stated how they plan to deploy the $150 million from the Vive divestiture. While it would not be ideal for my bull thesis, the $150 million does provide management with flexibility if they expect GMV to decline.

Conclusion

I am bullish on PROG. I will be adding to my existing position to dollar-cost average. For those thinking about starting a position, PROG Holdings has dividend and share buyback protection. PROG’s stock price should appreciate over the next 3-5 years due to buybacks increasing EPS growth and the triple-digit growth of Four. However, PROG is not a growth stock. If you are an investor who is chasing the next 100-bagger, PROG is not for you. If you are an investor who is looking for a ~95% return over the next 3-5 years while being paid a dividend, PROG Holdings, Inc. is for you.

data.title: Tariffs, Rates, Labor: The Big Questions Facing Canada's Economy
data.description:
A new report from TD Economics delves into the biggest questions facing Canada’s economy. Derek Burleton, VP and Deputy Chief Economist with TD Bank Group joins MoneyTalk to discuss the potential impact of tariffs, immigration and the Bank of Canada on Canada’s economic outlook.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: From the impact of the new federal budget to trade tensions, and a Bank of Canada which may be on hold for all of 2026, there are plenty of big questions facing our economy. Joining us now with some of the answers is Derek Burleton, VP and deputy chief economist with TD Bank Group.

Derek, always great to have you on the program. Welcome back.

Derek Burleton: Thank you, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: So, your shop, TD Economics, has put out a new report trying to answer some of these big questions facing Canada right now. Let's start with the federal budget. Now that it's law, will this start to transform the Canadian economy?

Derek Burleton: Yeah, we're going to have to wait and see. Rome wasn't built in a day. And Canada's facing huge challenges. I'll start off with things I liked about the budget-- a number of things.

I liked the fact it was focused on investment and crystalizing about $500 billion of private investment over the next five years. They set some ambitious targets. That's all great.

A lot of measures within that budget, I think, will be a good first step to moving the goalpost towards those objectives-- the public infrastructure investment, some of the tax initiatives, full expensing of capital cost, and a broadening of that measure that was during the Trudeau government. They've extended that and expanded it.

And so, those are good. Now, is it transformational? I'm going to hold my breath a bit on that one. I think, again, this is one budget. They're going to have other opportunities.

I would like to see deeper rethinks of the corporate tax system. There's a lot of regulatory-- I mean, when you think of Canada, just the steady growth in regulations and the costs, so much work to do on that front.

And I think they're going to have to be a bit bolder, right? For example, program spending review-- I think they could go deeper on that than they did. So we'll have to wait and see.

I think we're going to keep pushing them on being a bit bolder, on going further on some of these transformational issues. We are moving part of the way there, and we're going to be watching to see some of the near-term impacts.

Greg Bonnell: I can't think of a budget in recent years that have had this much expectation heading into it-- based, of course, on the fact that our trade relationship with the United States got pretty tense and didn't really get much better as we headed through this. This budget was supposed to be the thing that will chart a new course. What does that trade relationship like right now?

Next year, of course, we're going to have NAFTA, CUSMA, USMCA-- whatever you want to call it-- renegotiations. What does the relationship look like for you?

Derek Burleton: Well, right now, not much seems to be going on. I know the talks are always ongoing, but what we did do-- because we don't have a lot of sense, we don't have a lot of direction right now-- is we just assume the status quo in our forecasts through '26-'27 in terms of our current thinking.

That is still tariffs, still that USMCA exemption that's cushioning a lot of Canadian exports from duties. And we'll see. I mean, I'm comfortable with that view.

Now the big questions remain. Are we going to see a bilateral deal in the near term, which would offer some relief to sectors that have been hit, like steel and aluminum? I think there's a decent chance. But again, nothing's imminent there.

And then going beyond that USMCA review, when we look around the world, the kind of deals the US has been signing gives us a bit of a taste. Some base tariff, some relief, some quotas, some commitments from Canada to invest in the United States-- things that Mark Carney has flagged in some of his recent discussions about investing up to $1 trillion in the US over five years.

So we'll have to wait and see. We don't make any heroic assumptions in terms of our current view as we sort through some of these questions. But, I think I am more confident we're not going to see some of the disaster scenarios play out, just given the fact we've come this far without the full negative effects of tariffs that were initially feared.

Greg Bonnell: I could definitely use a few less disaster scenarios heading into 2026, so I like the sound of that.

Another big change in terms of the Canadian economy and our policy has been immigration. Obviously, a big part of the economic story of the last several years has been booming immigration. Those numbers have been peeled back. What kind of impact does that have?

Derek Burleton: Well, that was a shock we were talking about a year ago ahead of the-- before Trump took office-- and the impacts of going from 3% population growth effectively down to 0 and what kind of impact that could have on the economy.

We are starting to see some effects as that growth rate has slowed dramatically and will continue to slow well into 2026, taking some pressure off social infrastructure, easing some of the conditions in rental markets. We're seeing markets like Toronto, Vancouver that tend to have higher exposure to new international migrants. Students and the like are seeing some of the greatest easing show up. And I expect we're going to continue to see this.

One thing, it's a bit of a silver lining-- and I think 2026 should show this first-hand-- is that the unemployment rate has been rising gradually, albeit it's flattened at around 7% more recently. But the big growth in immigration labor force was really being the driver of that higher unemployment rate. Just the fact a lot of migrants weren't able to find jobs as we move into the early part of this year.

With that channel being choked off, I do see it capping the unemployment rate at just over 7%. Employment's not going to grow very much, but nor will the labor force. And we should start to see a pivot down in the unemployment rate. So that's actually a silver lining to some of the negative impacts on growth and labor force and the overall market.

Greg Bonnell: Now, this is our backdrop. We have a Bank of Canada that's indicated pretty firmly that it's on hold for now. Of course, then the question becomes, what does "for now" mean? Are they on hold for all of 2026? What could make the move in 2026?

Derek Burleton: Well, our baseline assumption is, yeah, no further moves. We're kind of at the low end of their neutral range. And I think barring a recession, which will certainly provoke more rate cuts, I think they're just going to be moving sideways. And right now, they're looking at a rate of underlying inflation count of about 2.5%. It's about half point too rich for where they'd like to see it.

The economy is still growing, albeit modestly. And I think they're very comfortable. And if you don't see any big deviation from this-- and we don't see inflation coming going down to 1 and 1/2 or anything-- I think they're just going to sit tight at that rate of 2 and a quarter.

Yields are going to be pretty flat as well when you look further up the yield curve. So I think Canadians have to prepare for a very stable rate environment. Could be worse. We've seen rates higher, but if you're looking for rates to come down a lot further, five-year yields and the like, you're just probably not going to see that.

Greg Bonnell: So if we're not banking on, obviously, there's been relief from the Bank of Canada that they've done their part. Now we actually have someone watching our conversation in real-time asking about productivity growth. You and I have talked about this a lot in Canada. We are concerned that we just didn't have it compared to other advanced economies. So this person basically asking, what structural reforms, investments do you see as most critical to actually get our productivity growing?

Derek Burleton: I think a lot of what was in the budget will help. And again, we're just going to hold out and watch and wait. We're not going to make any heroic assumptions that investing in ports and rail and transportation infrastructure is going to lead to some big productivity boom. Over time, I do anticipate some of that to show through.

I think one of the bigger areas of uncertainty is AI. And something we didn't see a lot of in the budget on AI, and that's something I'd like to see more of. We're going to be prodding again the federal government to focus more on AI strategy. But I do know that business sector is rolling out AI, and some of those benefits, I think, will flow through.

And we're looking at 1.5% growth in Canada. When you've got pretty much no growth in the labor force, you really are relying on productivity to step up a bit to achieve that. So we are making some assumptions, but it's still an area of big upside opportunity for Canada. No productivity boom on the horizon.

Greg Bonnell: If someone had to take away one big thought from our conversation here about 2026, the Canadian economy-- what should it be?

Derek Burleton: I think expecting more of the same in terms of growth, stable rates as we've talked about. I think the more interesting aspects are going to be what plays out in the US and globally. There's a lot of uncertainty with Trump now turning his attention to dealing with affordability. Could be good news for Canada on a tariff perspective as they try and limit some of the import cost increases.

But it could change the dynamic of the US economy, as well as some of the ideas on $2,000 checks to American households and the like to help them over the hump. So, I think for me, just like this year, a lot of the focus is going to be what happens south of the border in terms of how some of these issues play out.

Original Post

data.title: Trying To Give A Credit Rating For Sound Point Meridian Capital Inc
data.description:
In the following lines, we continue to look in more detail at the world of CLO CEF funds. Today, we will once again try to enter the role of Moody's and give a conditional credit rating to a company whose main exposure is to CLO equity tranches. We are talking about Sound Point Meridian Capital Inc (SPMC). We will analyze the fund and assign it a rating, but not according to the methodology adopted for CEF structures. And if you are wondering why - even though SPMC is a CEF - the reason is simple: Moody's does not have a developed framework for CLO CEF funds. The closest analogy is their methodology for BDC companies, which is why we use it. Of course, the obtained ratings should be accepted with the proviso that SPMC is not a BDC, and the comparison is indicative, aided by the observed correlation between the BDC and CLO CEF segments.

Basic Info

  • Total assets: around $541 million.
  • Current market capitalization: close to $324 million.
  • Portfolio:

- diversified portfolio of 94 CLOs.

- Below are shown the top 10 holdings of the CEF:

  • Expense ratio: 8.9%.
  • Non-leverage expense ratio: 6%.
  • Annual Expenses:
  • Historical performance:
  • The common stock is currently trading at $14.69, with a close to 9% discount:

The Fund's Main Beneficiary

Below, we can find the last available statement of operations:

In short, the fund generates close to 15% in current income, and around 30% of that income goes to various fees. Advisory fees and incentive fees are based on net investment income, and for the fund managers, it does not matter that the fund has lost close to 74% of its total investment income in realized and unrealized losses. Interest expense is around 15% of total investment income. Given the large expected losses in a CLO equity portfolio, the reader may ask: Who is the bagholder in this business, and who benefits the most? Managers are benefiting from investing in high-income (high-loss) securities, earning fees based on NII. Debt and preferred stockholders are safe as long as the fund maintains its regulatory leverage limit and generates enough cash to pay interest, and the buffer against all portfolio problems is the constant NAV erosion, well-documented in the sector.

Preferred Stocks

SPMC has two preferred stocks: (SPMA) and (SPME). Both are trading around par. SPME's call date has passed. SPMA has close to 1 year to call with a call date on 11/30/2026, and 4 years to maturity with a maturity date on 11/30/2029. Its spread to the 5-year treasury yield is around 3.69%, and currently it is close to 4.9%. Its yield to worst is around 8.12%.

We are facing a lot of questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is supposed to be the most precise way when dealing with ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate. Below, you can see different yields to worst vs prices of SPMA:

Below are shown the BDC over-the-counter bonds that mature in 3 to 5 years, similar to SPMA:

The largest yield to maturity is around 7.8% average, which is less than SPMA's YTW.

Improvised Credit Score For SPMC

Moody's BDC approach:

1. Profitability.

Moody’s looks at the average 3-year ratio between net income and assets under management. For SPMC, this indicator is about 2%, but we use a 2-year one, as the fund’s inception date is 2024, which, on their scale, results in a rating of 8, equivalent to Baa1.

2. Leverage and asset coverage ratio cushion (ACRC).

CEF funds have a regulatory asset coverage requirement of 200%. SPMC maintains about 27% leverage and an ACRC of 41.5%. This leads to a credit rating of Aa3. Keep in mind that here we use the overall leverage limit, which includes debt and preferred stock. We also depend on the company's willingness to keep leverage below 35% as stated in their last report:

As of September 30, 2025, we had debt outstanding which totaled approximately 33% of total assets. Over time and under normal market conditions, the Company expects to employ leverage within a range of 30% to 35% of total assets, although the actual amount of leverage will vary over time. As market conditions change, the Company may incur leverage outside of this range, subject to applicable regulatory and contractual limits.

3. Asset Quality.

The fund has no problem loans and senior secured loans, but it does lose a large portion of its investment income. Since the portfolio is almost entirely composed of CLO equity, we assign the lowest possible score for these metrics, or a credit rating of Ca.

4. Cash flow and liquidity.

SPMC has secured debt of about 14%, which indicates a credit rating of A3. The fund's core assets generate high income, providing sufficient liquidity to cover the extreme management and incentive fees. The 2 preferred stocks have maturity dates beyond 2029, further reducing the fund's liquidity needs. The use of secured debt is moderate and might be viewed as a positive; a person with a credit card history is viewed as more credible than a person without one.

Overall Score

The aggregation of the scores yields a conditional overall rating of around Baa2. After adjustments for market capitalization, discount/premium to NAV, historical forward yield, and high expenses, the final result shifts to a Baa3 credit rating.

Conclusion

SPMC is a relatively new CEF CLO that aims to tap into the public's willingness to pay high fees for high current income from investments in CLO equity. Our general view of the business model is that it primarily benefits the fund managers while outsourcing all risk to equity holders. The fixed-income instruments are the only way to benefit from the structure, as their return potential is relatively safe given regulatory leverage limitations. Based on our improvised approach, the company's debt would receive a credit score very close to investment-grade. The term preferred stock would most likely be below investment-grade. This is evident from their high nominal yields. We view the fixed-income in CEF CLO as tempting, but it definitely needs monitoring given the extreme capital losses in the underlying portfolio during market turbulence.

data.title: Arteris: The Hidden Gem Powering The Chiplet Revolution
data.description:
Arteris, Inc. (AIP) specializes in providing Network on Chip (NOC) solutions and selling intellectual property and software that help semiconductor companies design complex chips efficiently. Its products include FlexNoC 5, which is the backbone of NoC, NCore that ensures cache coherency; and Magillem, which is a tool for assembling chip components.

For decades, the semiconductor industry has been relying on Moore's Law, as the number of transistors doubles every two years. A large chip was manufactured to include the CPU, GPU, and even memory, but after reaching 3- and 2-nanometer chips, the chip manufacturing cost became very high, and on top of that, it could be unusable due to a single speck of dust. The industry saw that the solution is to cut the chip into smaller pieces called Chiplets. For example, a piece of the CPU, another for the GPU, and so on. They then combine these pieces together like LEGO bricks.

However, a problem arose, which is, how would the data from each piece communicate efficiently with the others?. This is where the Interconnect role comes in. Interconnect is a complex network of routers and switches inside the chip and costs up to $500,000, not to mention the engineers and the risk of design failure. Arteris offers the solution, as it sells the Intellectual Property (IP) blueprint of the interconnect network, allowing companies to focus on the chip's internal components.

FlexNoC 5

Arteris offers a range of products; the first one is FlexNoC 5, which is the backbone of its ecosystem. The company added in its fifth version a feature called Physical Awareness. In the past, the Logic designer was designing the network on paper, but when they went to the implementation engineer, they discovered errors, such as the wires' length being incompatible with chip components, which forced them to redesign everything. However, with FlexNoC 5, which understands the chip physics from the beginning, it solved this problem. Furthermore, this version supports complex network topologies, such as Mesh and Torus, that are used to train the AI models with data width up to 1024 bits to transfer AI data quickly.

NCore

Ncore's function is to ensure the cache coherency process. Imagine that there are three employees who work on the same Excel file, and each employee has a temporary copy of it on his computer (this is the cache). If an employee modified the file and these changes didn't reach the others, the copies would be different, leading to errors. Ncore makes sure that all CPUs and Accelerators on the chip receive a single, correct, and updated copy of the data. Importantly, Ncore can connect any CPU type to any Accelerator, unlike competitors like ARM, which have similar solutions (CoreLink) but work better with ARM-based designs.

Magillem

In 2020, Arteris acquired Magillem Design Services to solve the assembly problem. Magillem is a software that helps engineers in assembling the parts of the chip with each other like a puzzle game, saving 30% of design time and reducing critical errors.

Partnerships and Clients

Arteris joined the UALink Consortium with giants like Intel and Meta to develop AI Accelerators standards, which is a quality seal on its products and also protects them from these companies making a closed system. Therefore, Arteris wouldn't find itself outside the field since it is one of the players setting the rules.

Additionally, the company entered the large FPGA market through making a deal with Altera (Intel FPGA solution), licensing its products, including FlexGen and NCore, in their new FPGA chips, which shows great confidence in their products.

Moreover, among the company's clients is Whalechip, which is a promising Chinese company in Near Memory Computing and chose to rely on the FlexNoC 5 solution despite geopolitical tensions, indicating that this technology has no alternative in their region.

Financials

During Q3 of 2025, the company made revenues of $17.4 million (18% YoY). Importantly, the Annual Contract Value (ACV) and Royalties reached $74.9 million (24% YoY), meaning that the company has begun to build a solid revenue base. Also, the Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) reached $104.7 million (34% YoY), which is a healthy sign that indicates the future contracts are quickly growing, which clarifies the future vision for investors in the next few years and reduces the revenue volatility.

Arteris' non-GAAP gross margin reached 90%, meaning that 90 cents of every additional dollar from sales will convert into gross profit, covering operating expenses. So, the profit is expected to jump once the revenues cover the fixed costs, such as employee salaries.

The company recorded $8.7 million in operating losses during Q3, but after excluding stock-based compensation, the loss decreased to $3.5 million. However, Arteris started to generate positive free cash flow in some quarters, including this quarter, with FCF of $2.5 million. This is a turning point that proves the company can finance itself without relying on cash burn.

In general, Arteris's financial position is relatively secure, as it has a liquidity of $56 million with a decreasing burn rate, suggesting that it wouldn't need to raise capital in the short term unless it entered into a large acquisition.

Guidance

The management's comments expect the momentum to continue in the next quarter and full year, as they raised their full-year guidance to around $69 million in revenues and $74-$78 million for ACV + Royalties. Most importantly, they expect the year to end with a positive FCF of $2.5-5.5 million, indicating a shift towards self-financing and operational efficiency, driven by a 34% RPO growth YoY.

Valuation

Arteris expects revenues of $69 million in 2025, and it has a TTM EV/Sales of approximately 8x. However, the average multiple of its closest competitors, Synopsys (SNPS) and Cadence (CDNS), is 14x. I will assume a conservative scenario of 10x to put in a margin of safety. The enterprise value stands at approximately $690 million. With that in mind and after adding cash and equivalents of $56.2 million, the equity value comes to $746.2 million, or $17 per share (shares outstanding at 43.68 million).

Risks

The primary risk facing Arteris is its significant exposure to the Chinese market, which represents 24% of its revenues. It has also acknowledged relationships with Chinese companies such as HiSilicon and Bada, both of which have been blacklisted in the USA due to the US-China trade war. Therefore, any new export restrictions could significantly impact revenues. Management itself stated that it anticipates a decline in revenues coming from China.

In addition, companies like Apple or Google may decide to design their own solutions instead of relying on third parties. Therefore, Arteris must continue to innovate to demonstrate that purchasing their services is a more profitable option than in-house manufacturing.

Another risk is the company's concentration on a small number of major players. Losing even one of these customers could lead to sharp fluctuations in the stock price.

Conclusion

Investing in Arteris is a strategic bet on the complexity of the semiconductor industry. The more difficult the chip manufacturing process, the greater the need for Arteris services. In my opinion, if we take into account the current stock's low price of $13 plus the projections that expect the chiplet market to reach $157 billion by 2030 and the strong economic moat the company has thanks to its FlexNoC 5 and NCore technologies that are difficult to imitate, I would give it a buy rating, provided that it does not exceed 5% of the portfolio to hedge against China risks.
